title: A hybrid IFENN solver for generalizable modeling of phase-field fracture initiation and propagation
authors: Panos Pantidis, Fouad Amin, Diab Abueidda, Mostafa E. Mobasher
abstract: In this paper we demonstrate how the Integrated Finite Element Neural Network (IFENN) framework can effectively model the entire evolution of phase-field fracture, including the initiation and propagation stage, across generalizable geometries. IFENN is a hybrid scheme for coupled computational mechanics problems, tightly coupling a standard FEM solver (mechanical equilibrium) with a pre-trained neural network (coupled field). In this work, the phase-field diffusion equation is approximated with: i) a DeepONet architecture with Kolmogorov-Arnold networks in the trunk and branch (DeepOKAN) for the initiation stage, and ii) a Convolution Neural Network (CNN) for the propagation stage. Both networks are trained only once, on a benchmark geometry, using a purely physics-informed approach based on the maximum strain energy and the phase-field variable. The training process utilizes an extremely small number of training increments and only a limited number of Gauss points that are strategically sampled from the fracture process zone. These features enable a substantial decrease of the offline training cost. To address the extrapolation of the DeepOKAN predictions in regions away from the crack tip during the inference stage, we implement a set of artificial boundary conditions to enforce the near-zero values in the far-field predictions. We showcase the flexibility and numerical accuracy of the proposed methodology across both the training and unseen geometries.

infographic.key_insight: IFENN couples FEM with a pre-trained neural operator to model both initiation and propagation of phase-field fracture across unseen geometries.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables faster offline training and generalizable fracture prediction for solid mechanics applications with reduced sampling cost.
method: DeepOKAN predicts phase-field evolution during crack initiation using physics-informed training.
A CNN handles the propagation stage after initiation.
Artificial boundary conditions suppress unrealistic far-field DeepOKAN extrapolation.
infographic.evidence_signal: The method is trained once on a benchmark geometry with only a few increments and limited Gauss-point samples, then evaluated on both trained and unseen geometries.
infographic.watch_item: Accuracy may depend on careful selection of fracture-process-zone samples and on the artificial boundary conditions used to control extrapolation.
infographic.paper_title: A hybrid IFENN solver for generalizable modeling of phase-field fracture initiation and propagation

title: Physics-Informed Neural Network with Transfer Learning for State Estimation in Lithium-Ion Batteries using the Single Particle Model with Electrolyte
authors: Gift Modekwe, Qiugang Lu
abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have emerged as a powerful tool for solving nonlinear partial differential equations (PDEs), including battery electrochemical models. They typically en-force conservation laws within the loss function to ensure physically consistent solutions. Tradi-tional numerical methods such as finite difference, finite volume, and finite element techniques, re-ly on discretization and can be computationally expensive for nonlinear systems. To address this challenge, PINNs offer improved scalability, particularly for reduced-order models like the single particle model with electrolyte (SPMe). The SPMe describes lithium-ion battery dynamics through coupled diffusion, transport, reaction kinetics, and voltage equations. Despite these advantages, training SPMe-based PINNs from scratch for different battery chemistries or operating conditions is demanding and often leads to slow convergence. To overcome this limitation, this work introduces a transfer learning framework for SPMe-PINNs. The model is first pretrained to learn general elec-trochemical dynamics and then adapted to a target battery by transferring weights, freezing se-lected layers, and fine tuning the remaining parameters, including estimating key electrochemical variables. Validation using PyBaMM demonstrates accurate voltage prediction, indicating that the proposed approach preserves electrochemical consistency while reducing training time and ena-bling efficient generalization across batteries.

infographic.key_insight: A transfer-learned physics-informed neural network can estimate lithium-ion battery states accurately by reusing electrochemical knowledge across different operating conditions and chemistries.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This approach can cut training cost and improve scalability for battery state estimation while preserving the physics needed for reliable digital-twin and BMS applications.
method: Pretrain a PINN on the single particle model with electrolyte (SPMe) to learn general electrochemical behavior.
Transfer the learned weights to a target battery, freeze selected layers, and fine-tune the remaining parameters.
Estimate key electrochemical states while enforcing conservation laws and voltage consistency in the loss function.
infographic.evidence_signal: Validation with PyBaMM shows accurate voltage prediction, suggesting the transfer-learning PINN maintains electrochemical consistency while improving training efficiency.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on how well the pretrained source battery matches the target battery, and layer-freezing choices can affect convergence and accuracy.
infographic.paper_title: Physics-Informed Neural Network with Transfer Learning for State Estimation in Lithium-Ion Batteries using the Single Particle Model with Electrolyte

title: Uniaxial compression of crystalline HCP titanium: an atomistic modelling study of size effects
authors: Fatemeh Safari, Konstantinos Konstantinou
abstract: Understanding the deformation behaviour of titanium is important not only for technological advances associated with industrially-relevant applications, but also essential to achieve a fundamental understanding of the mechanical properties of the relevant alloys. In this computational study, molecular-dynamics simulations are employed to ascertain the impact of model size on the mechanical response of alpha-titanium under compression. The deformation behaviour of the crystalline models is investigated as a function of different system sizes (varied by four orders of magnitude, up to 32 million atoms), and strain rates (down to 10^8 s^-1). The results show that the elastic properties remain independent of both system size and strain rate, whereas marked size effects emerge during plastic deformation. Increasing the system size of the titanium model reduces stress fluctuations, results in more homogeneous structural evolution, and stabilizes dislocation activity. Decreasing the applied strain rate requires correspondingly a larger system size to achieve equilibration and to ensure a stable behaviour for the simulated structure. The modelling results demonstrate that system size and strain rate are strongly coupled, and their combined effect governs the simulated deformation behaviour of the compressed crystalline material.

infographic.key_insight: Elastic response of HCP titanium is size- and strain-rate independent, but plastic deformation becomes strongly size dependent, with larger models producing more stable and homogeneous behavior.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For atomistic compression studies of titanium, model size must be matched to strain rate to obtain reliable plasticity predictions and avoid misleading fluctuations in the simulated response.
method: Molecular-dynamics simulations of crystalline alpha-titanium under uniaxial compression
System size varied across four orders of magnitude, up to 32 million atoms
Strain rate studied down to 10^8 s^-1
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports that elastic properties were unchanged by system size or strain rate, while larger systems reduced stress fluctuations and stabilized dislocation activity during plastic deformation.
infographic.watch_item: Very small cells or overly fast loading rates can under-resolve plastic mechanisms, so size–rate coupling must be checked before trusting deformation results.
infographic.paper_title: Uniaxial compression of crystalline HCP titanium: an atomistic modelling study of size effects

title: A robust mixed finite element formulation for third medium contact
authors: M. Vorwerk, J. Schröder, P. Wriggers
abstract: Third medium contact provides a smooth continuum alternative to classical contact algorithms by replacing explicit contact constraints with a highly compliant fictitious medium. In this work, an auxiliary-field stabilization is introduced in which a deformation-gradient-like field is treated as an independent unknown in the third medium and coupled to the physical deformation gradient by a penalty term. A gradient contribution acting on the auxiliary field provides the regularization mechanism without requiring a direct evaluation of higher displacement derivatives. Linear and quadratic interpolation spaces are investigated, including continuous and element-wise discontinuous auxiliary-field approximations. The numerical results show that continuous low-order auxiliary fields provide an effective gradient-type stabilization of the third medium, even when the displacement field is approximated by first-order finite elements. For element-wise discontinuous auxiliary fields, the additional unknowns remain local to each element and can be eliminated locally by static condensation, so that the global system does not necessarily contain additional auxiliary degrees of freedom. Benchmark problems involving large deformation, progressive self-contact and severe third-medium compression are used to assess the formulation.

infographic.key_insight: The paper stabilizes third-medium contact by introducing an auxiliary deformation-gradient-like field with gradient regularization, avoiding direct higher-derivative displacement calculations.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This makes smooth, robust contact modeling more practical for large-deformation and self-contact problems while keeping the formulation compatible with standard finite elements.
method: Treat the deformation-gradient-like quantity as an independent auxiliary unknown in the third medium.
Couple auxiliary and physical deformation gradients through a penalty term.
Add a gradient term on the auxiliary field; support continuous or element-wise discontinuous interpolation, with local static condensation for the discontinuous case.
infographic.evidence_signal: The formulation is evaluated on benchmark problems featuring large deformation, progressive self-contact, and severe third-medium compression, with results reported for linear and quadratic interpolation spaces.
infographic.watch_item: The stabilization strength and accuracy likely depend on penalty/regularization choices and on the selected auxiliary-field interpolation, so parameter tuning and conditioning need care.
infographic.paper_title: A robust mixed finite element formulation for third medium contact

title: From Weights to Features: SAE-Guided Activation Regularization for LLM Continual Learning
authors: Evan Ning, Wei Xue, Dong Lou, Yike Guo
abstract: Weight-space regularization methods such as Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) are the standard approach to catastrophic forgetting in continual learning. However, those methods tend to underperform when applied to large language models. We argue that such underperformance can be partly explained by the ``polysemantic'' nature of large language models: per-weight importance estimates utilized by EWC-style regularization are too coarse and cannot isolate the knowledge that needs protection. In this paper, we propose regularizing instead in the model's activation space, using pretrained Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) as a monosemantic feature dictionary. From the perspective of constrained optimization, we derive a new loss function that uses the SAE feature dictionary to explicitly balance stability and plasticity, and show that EWC is a special case in the one-sided weight-space penalty setting. Unlike replay-based methods that store or revisit examples from earlier tasks, our method requires no previous-task data after mask construction: current-task data is used to compute a compact SAE feature mask, and only this mask is retained for later training. Further, since the feature space has significantly lower dimensionality than the parameter space, the proposed method is more memory efficient. On the TRACE and MedCL continual learning benchmarks, the method achieves the strongest result among approaches without introducing task-specific architectural components, also surpassing traditional weight-space regularization methods like EWC. Beyond performance comparisons, we provide empirical evidence for the polysemanticity thesis: task-relevant representations are linearly separable in the SAE feature basis but indistinguishable from chance in the weight basis, and weight-space protection is nearly non-selective at the concept level.

infographic.key_insight: Protecting knowledge in LLM continual learning works better in sparse activation feature space than in raw weight space, because SAE features isolate task-relevant information more cleanly.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The method offers a memory-efficient, no-replay alternative to weight regularization for continual adaptation of large language models while reducing catastrophic forgetting.
method: Use pretrained sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to build a monosemantic feature dictionary.
Construct a compact task-specific SAE feature mask from current-task data only.
Regularize activations in feature space to balance stability and plasticity, with EWC emerging as a special weight-space case.
infographic.evidence_signal: On TRACE and MedCL continual learning benchmarks, the method outperforms traditional weight-space regularization such as EWC and is strongest among non-task-specific architectures.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on the quality and availability of pretrained SAEs and on how well the feature mask captures task-relevant concepts.
infographic.paper_title: From Weights to Features: SAE-Guided Activation Regularization for LLM Continual Learning

title: Mapping Political-Elite Networks in Europe with a Multilingual Joint Entity-Relation Extraction Pipeline
authors: Kirill Solovev, Jana Lasser
abstract: Whether political elites organise into rent-seeking coalitions that capture public resources or civic networks that sustain governance is a central question in comparative politics. Yet observing these complex, informal, and adversarial ties at scale has historically required intensive manual coding, while automated text-as-data methods have largely been limited to simple co-occurrence. Recent large language model (LLM) approaches offer a path forward but often rely on proprietary APIs, lack cross-lingual capability, and struggle with scalable entity resolution. We present a modular, fully open-weight pipeline for multilingual joint entity-relation extraction that builds signed, temporal knowledge graphs from massive unstructured news corpora. It combines span-based named-entity recognition (NER) with a three-stage linking cascade mapping mentions to language-independent Wikidata identifiers; a high-throughput, ontology-constrained mixture-of-experts model then uses guided decoding to extract directed, signed relationships grounded in a domain ontology. A full-coverage spot-check against a 3491-relation gold standard shows high textual correctness (68.2% strict to 93.7% lenient). Two large-scale case studies validate the pipeline against the public record. In Austria, it reconstructs a political party's complete lifecycle, dating internal fractures and tracking personnel into successor factions and court convictions. In a Polish corpus, it uncovers the overlapping economic and governance networks of state-enterprise patronage, alongside the structurally balanced, signed conflict network of the polarized Civic Platform (Platforma Obywatelska, PO)--Law and Justice (Prawo i Sprawiedliwość, PiS) duopoly. By bridging raw multilingual text and structured relational data, our framework provides a robust, replicable foundation for cross-national empirical computational social science.

infographic.key_insight: The paper presents a fully open, multilingual pipeline that turns unstructured news text into signed, temporal political network graphs by jointly extracting entities and relations at scale.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It shows how guided decoding, ontology constraints, and entity linking can make large-scale information extraction more robust and reproducible for computational social science workflows.
method: Span-based NER identifies person and organization mentions in multiple languages.
A three-stage linking cascade resolves mentions to language-independent Wikidata IDs.
A high-throughput mixture-of-experts extractor produces directed, signed relations under a domain ontology.
infographic.evidence_signal: Against a 3,491-relation gold standard, the pipeline achieved 68.2% strict and 93.7% lenient textual correctness, and case studies in Austria and Poland matched known political events and networks.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on ontology quality and entity resolution accuracy, so errors in linking or relation constraints can propagate into the final knowledge graph.
infographic.paper_title: Mapping Political-Elite Networks in Europe with a Multilingual Joint Entity-Relation Extraction Pipeline

title: Can Scale Save Us From Plasticity Loss in Large Language Models?
authors: J. Fernando Hernandez-Garcia, Tomás Figliolia, Beren Millidge
abstract: The loss of plasticity - the ability of a network to learn new information after having already learned older information - is a fundamental challenge in creating artificial neural networks capable of continual learning. Although this phenomenon has been known for decades, it has mostly been studied in older, relatively small architectures and rarely in natural-language domains. To determine whether loss of plasticity remains a problem in the modern transformer-based LLM paradigm, we study plasticity loss in GPT-style Transformer models trained on a multilingual continual learning problem. Consistent with prior work, we find evidence of plasticity loss across models ranging from 5M to 314M non-embedding parameters, as measured by deterioration on a held-out Vietnamese probing task. We further find that the onset of plasticity loss follows a predictable scaling law, growing sublinearly with model size. These results suggest that larger models may delay the measurable effects of plasticity loss, but that increasing parameter count alone is likely to be insufficient to completely prevent it. We also find evidence of plasticity loss under stationary multilingual training, challenging the view that the phenomenon is exclusive to continual learning with abrupt task changes. Overall, our results suggest that even large Transformer language models trained on natural-language will eventually lose the ability to efficiently adapt to new data after sufficiently long training, in both continual and stationary settings.

infographic.key_insight: Scaling up GPT-style language models delays plasticity loss, but does not eliminate it, and even stationary training can eventually reduce a model’s ability to learn new information.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For continual-learning and long-horizon deployment of LLMs, bigger models alone are not a reliable fix; maintaining adaptability likely requires training or architectural interventions beyond parameter scaling.
method: Studied GPT-style Transformers on a multilingual continual-learning setup.
Compared models from 5M to 314M non-embedding parameters.
Tracked plasticity using performance deterioration on a held-out Vietnamese probing task, including stationary multilingual training as a control.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a clear deterioration on a held-out Vietnamese probe across model sizes, with the onset of plasticity loss following a sublinear scaling law.
infographic.watch_item: The results suggest that scale only delays plasticity loss, so practitioners should not assume larger LLMs will remain equally adaptable over long training or deployment.
infographic.paper_title: Can Scale Save Us From Plasticity Loss in Large Language Models?

title: Droplet Fusion as a Relaxation Process: Comparison with Shape Recovery of Newtonian and Viscoelastic Droplets
authors: Mohammad Moein Naderi, Zhangli Peng, Huan-Xiang Zhou
abstract: Biomolecular condensates formed by phase separation often exhibit viscoelastic behavior, yet their shape recovery and fusion dynamics are frequently interpreted using purely viscous models. Here, we develop a unified theoretical and computational framework to quantify how viscoelasticity governs these two processes. We combine analytical theory for small-deformation shape recovery with axisymmetric finite-element simulations based on the Oldroyd-B constitutive model to systematically investigate both shape recovery and droplet fusion under comparable conditions. Our results show that, although both processes are driven by capillary forces, they are fundamentally distinct in their underlying physics. Shape recovery is governed by global viscocapillary relaxation of a single connected interface and follows single- or multi-exponential decay depending on the relative magnitude of the viscocapillary timescale and the stress relaxation time. In contrast, droplet fusion is intrinsically a multistage process involving localized curvature-driven neck formation, rapid bridge expansion, and a transition to global relaxation. We demonstrate that viscoelasticity introduces an additional intrinsic timescale that governs the competition between capillary driving and stress relaxation, characterized by the Deborah number. This leads to enhanced intermediate-stage fusion dynamics and modified relaxation behavior compared to Newtonian droplets. Furthermore, we show that the presence of an exterior fluid introduces additional hydrodynamic dissipation, significantly slowing the fusion process. Finally, we compare the computationally predicted droplet fusion in the Newtonian and viscoelastic cases with a stretched-exponential empirical formula. Deviations observed in viscoelastic regimes highlight the limitations of purely viscous descriptions and the need for models incorporating stress relaxation.

infographic.key_insight: Shape recovery and droplet fusion in biomolecular condensates are both capillary-driven, but viscoelastic stress relaxation makes them fundamentally different relaxation processes.
infographic.engineering_relevance: Models for condensate dynamics must include stress relaxation and surrounding-fluid dissipation to predict fusion and recovery times accurately in soft-matter and biomedical systems.
method: Analytical small-deformation theory for single-droplet shape recovery
Axisymmetric finite-element simulations using the Oldroyd-B constitutive model
Systematic comparison of Newtonian vs. viscoelastic droplets under matched conditions
infographic.evidence_signal: The paper combines theory and simulation and reports that viscoelastic droplets show multistage fusion, enhanced intermediate-stage dynamics, and deviations from stretched-exponential fits relative to Newtonian cases.
infographic.watch_item: The Oldroyd-B model and stretched-exponential fits may miss additional material nonlinearities or interfacial effects in real biomolecular condensates.
infographic.paper_title: Droplet Fusion as a Relaxation Process: Comparison with Shape Recovery of Newtonian and Viscoelastic Droplets

title: Mechanical response of quasi-two-dimensional colloidal clusters under uniaxial tension
authors: Yanhui Yang, Jiawei Kang, Yao Li, Xiaoguang Ma
abstract: Despite extensive studies of equilibrium conformations of colloidal clusters, little is known about their mechanical response. Here, we investigate the tensile behavior of a quasi-two-dimensional colloidal cluster subjected to uniaxial tension up to fracture. The sample is a ribbon-shaped assembly of 16 colloidal beads bound by short-range depletion attraction. Using multiple optical tweezers, we clamp the cluster at both ends and perform a tensile test along its long axis. Combining video microscopy with particle tracking, we measure the tensile stress, strain, and particle configurations during deformation. We observe diverse mechanical response behaviors, including elastic, plastic, and soft-mode deformation, with fracture occurring at a strain near 10\%. To explain these behaviors, we construct a spring-mass frame model with breakable elastic bonds. We perform canonical Monte Carlo simulations on the full model with 32 degrees of freedom and compute the statistical distributions of mechanical observables using a simplified model with only 7 degrees of freedom. Both the simulations and the theoretical calculations accurately reproduce the experimental stress--strain curves. Moreover, the configuration distributions predicted by the simplified model agree well with both experiment and simulation in the elastic and soft-mode regimes, with only minor discrepancies in the plastic regime. This work demonstrates that the simplified spring-mass model captures the essential physics governing the rich tensile response behavior of the colloidal cluster.

infographic.key_insight: A quasi-2D colloidal cluster under uniaxial tension shows elastic, plastic, and soft-mode deformation before fracturing at about 10% strain, and a simplified spring-mass model captures this behavior.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The study provides a minimal mechanics model for predicting deformation and fracture in small bonded particle assemblies, useful for designing and understanding soft granular or colloidal materials.
method: Clamp a 16-bead ribbon-shaped colloidal cluster with optical tweezers and load it in uniaxial tension.
Track particle motion with video microscopy to extract stress, strain, and configuration changes during deformation.
Compare experiment against a breakable spring-mass frame model using Monte Carlo simulations and a reduced 7-DOF theory.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports that both the full simulations and simplified theoretical calculations reproduce the experimental stress-strain curves, and the simplified model matches observed configurations well in the elastic and soft-mode regimes.
infographic.watch_item: The reduced model shows minor discrepancies in the plastic regime, so plastic deformation details may require the full higher-dimensional model for accurate prediction.
infographic.paper_title: Mechanical response of quasi-two-dimensional colloidal clusters under uniaxial tension

title: A perfectly matched layer for damping vertically propagating waves in the compressible Boussinesq equations
authors: Timothy C. Andrews, Kenneth Duru, David Lee
abstract: This paper introduces a new application of the perfectly matched layer (PML) for mitigating model top wave reflections in geophysical fluid models. Typically, a strong Laplacian or Rayleigh damping sponge layer is used near the upper boundary, but these often need many vertical levels or a high model top to be sufficiently effective. An advantage of the PML is that, at the continuous level, it is free of wave reflection at the onset of the damping layer. This enables the PML to be effective even with a thin damping layer. We derive PMLs for the linear and nonlinear versions of the Boussinesq equations, which are a simplified model for vertical dynamics in the atmosphere. In the nonlinear system, we define a novel PML that damps perturbations from a hydrostatically balanced reference state. We approximate the PML equations using the compatible finite element method for numerical experiments. First, tests with the linear Boussinesq system show that the PML is more effective than a typical sponge layer in absorbing acoustic waves near the model top. Next, tests in the nonlinear system show that i) the PML can damp acoustic waves even when they are under-resolved by the time discretisation, and ii) the PML can avoid the standing wave pattern caused by model top reflection of orographic gravity waves. We propose that the PML is worth further development and investigation as a sponge layer alternative in dynamical cores for atmospheric modelling.

infographic.key_insight: A perfectly matched layer can suppress upper-boundary wave reflections in compressible Boussinesq models with much less damping thickness than traditional sponge layers.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This offers a more compact and potentially more effective boundary treatment for atmospheric dynamical cores that must absorb acoustic and gravity waves near the model top.
method: Derive PML formulations for both linear and nonlinear compressible Boussinesq equations.
Use a novel nonlinear PML that damps perturbations about a hydrostatically balanced reference state.
Discretize the PML with a compatible finite element method and test against sponge-layer baselines.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical experiments showing the PML outperforms a typical sponge layer for acoustic-wave absorption and removes standing-wave reflections from orographic gravity waves.
infographic.watch_item: The approach is demonstrated in idealized Boussinesq tests, so robustness, parameter tuning, and cost in full atmospheric models still need validation.
infographic.paper_title: A perfectly matched layer for damping vertically propagating waves in the compressible Boussinesq equations

title: Interpretable Concept-Guided Polynomial Tabular Kolmogorov-Arnold Network for EEG-Based Mild Cognitive Impairment Detection
authors: Yosef Bernardus Wirian, Qiang Cheng
abstract: Early and scalable detection of mild cognitive impairment (MCI) remains an unresolved clinical challenge. Existing EEG-based screening approaches are constrained by handcrafted feature pipelines that discard neurophysiologically meaningful domain structure and deep learning classifiers that sacrifice interpretability for performance. No existing work unifies physiologically organized concept encoders, cross-concept interaction modeling, and nonlinear tabular classification in a sleep EEG-based MCI detection framework. This study proposes Concept-guided Polynomial-transformed Tabular learning using Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (CPTabKAN), which maps heterogeneous EEG-derived features into domain-informed concept representations, expands them via degree-2 polynomial transformation to expose first- and second-order interactions, and applies a Fourier-parameterized TabKAN classifier to learn nonlinear decision boundaries. CPTabKAN was evaluated on the Study of Osteoporotic Fractures cohort (372 subjects, overnight polysomnography), using 1,379 features organized into ten physiologically motivated concept groups. Under 10-fold cross-validation, CPTabKAN-Second Order achieved a weighted F1-score of 0.9038 (SD 0.034), outperforming GradientBoosting by 5.65 percentage points (t(9)=1.934,p=0.043, one-sided paired test), with advantages persisting under SMOTE-based balancing. Ablation analysis confirmed independent contributions from each component. Concept importance analysis revealed that power spectral density, multi-scale entropy, and Hjorth parameters dominated first-order weights, while cross-concept interactions involving Lempel-Ziv-Welch complexity, statistics, demographics, and slow oscillations exceeded all first-order scores. These results demonstrate that concept-structured, interaction-aware tabular learning surfaces physiologically coherent reasoning, supporting clinical trust.

infographic.key_insight: CPTabKAN combines physiologically grouped EEG concepts with polynomial interaction modeling to improve interpretable MCI detection from sleep EEG.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The approach offers a clinically usable tabular-learning framework that preserves domain structure while exposing meaningful cross-feature interactions for decision support.
method: Organize 1,379 EEG-derived features into 10 physiologically motivated concept groups.
Apply degree-2 polynomial expansion to capture first- and second-order cross-concept interactions.
Use a Fourier-parameterized Kolmogorov-Arnold tabular classifier for nonlinear prediction and concept weighting.
infographic.evidence_signal: On the Study of Osteoporotic Fractures cohort (372 subjects), CPTabKAN-Second Order achieved a weighted F1 of 0.9038 under 10-fold cross-validation, outperforming GradientBoosting by 5.65 points.
infographic.watch_item: Results are promising, but the method depends on careful concept grouping and should be validated for generalization across other EEG cohorts, acquisition protocols, and class-imbalance settings.
infographic.paper_title: Interpretable Concept-Guided Polynomial Tabular Kolmogorov-Arnold Network for EEG-Based Mild Cognitive Impairment Detection

title: Direct stress imaging from shear wave propagation
authors: Yiwei Duan, Michel Destrade, Wenchang Tan, Guo-Yang Li
abstract: Quantitative imaging of stress fields in heterogeneous solids remains challenging because stress is not directly measurable and is typically inferred from deformation using constitutive models. Here we present Acoustoelastic Imaging (AEI), a non-destructive framework for reconstructing stress fields from shear wave propagation. AEI exploits the acoustoelastic effect, whereby pre-existing stress modifies local wave dynamics, and formulates stress recovery as an inverse problem of the governing wave equations. Using full shear waveform inversion with physics-informed learning, AEI reconstructs wave-equation coefficients from full-field wave measurements, enabling estimation of stress magnitude and principal directions without explicit constitutive model specification or material-parameter calibration. We demonstrate sub-wavelength spatial resolution (< 0.28 λ) and accurate reconstruction of nonuniform stress fields in heterogeneous materials through numerical simulations and ultrasound shear wave elastography experiments. These results establish a general framework for high-resolution stress imaging and provide a route toward non-invasive mapping of internal mechanical states in complex materials and biological tissues.

infographic.key_insight: Acoustoelastic Imaging reconstructs internal stress fields directly from shear-wave propagation by treating stress recovery as a physics-informed inverse wave problem.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables non-destructive, sub-wavelength stress mapping in heterogeneous solids and tissues without needing an explicit constitutive model or prior material calibration.
method: Measure full-field shear waveforms in the specimen.
Use physics-informed full waveform inversion to recover wave-equation coefficients altered by pre-stress.
Infer stress magnitude and principal directions from the reconstructed wave dynamics.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical simulations and ultrasound shear wave elastography experiments, achieving accurate nonuniform stress reconstruction with spatial resolution below 0.28λ.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on wavefield quality and inversion stability, so noisy measurements or strong modeling mismatch could limit robustness in real materials.
infographic.paper_title: Direct stress imaging from shear wave propagation

title: Primary damage and mechanical degradation of WTaCrV refractory high-entropy alloy: effects of solid-solution and chemical ordering
authors: Yihan Wu, Pengfei Yu, Yaohong Suo, Lei Zhang
abstract: As advanced nuclear reactors demand novel irradiation-tolerant materials, this study investigates the radiation damage and mechanical degradation of the promising WTaCrV refractory high-entropy alloy (RHEA). To isolate complex nanoscale chemical effects, we propose an atomistic modeling strategy comparing Average-Atom (AA), random solid-solution (RSS), and local chemical order (LCO) configurations using newly developed interatomic potentials. Collision cascades simulations reveal that the number of Frenkel pairs follow NRSS > NLCO > NAA at the same radiation dose. While the RSS effect accelerates defect generation due to rugged energy landscapes, LCO enhances lattice cohesion to mitigate radiation damage. Despite more primary defects in the RSS and LCO configurations compared with the AA configurations, the RSS and LCO effects can suppress radiation-induced mechanical degradation. Irradiation severely degrade the homogenized AA model but exert a limited impact on the strength and flow stress of the RSS and LCO models. This exceptional resistance is driven by inherent lattice distortion resulting from interactions among different alloy elements, which outweighs point defect induced lattice disruptions. Moreover, the complex interactions between deformation twins and point defects cause confined plastic flow, elevating flow stress in the RSS and LCO models. The findings provide atomistic guidance for performance assessment of next-generation structural materials for extreme nuclear environments.

infographic.key_insight: Random solid-solution and local chemical order in WTaCrV change how radiation damage and deformation evolve, with both configurations suppressing mechanical degradation despite forming more primary defects than the average-atom model.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For reactor-facing refractory alloys, nanoscale chemical disorder and ordering can improve irradiation tolerance and help retain strength under extreme nuclear environments.
method: Atomistic simulations with newly developed interatomic potentials
Compare Average-Atom (AA), random solid-solution (RSS), and local chemical order (LCO) configurations
Run collision-cascade and deformation analyses to track Frenkel pairs, twins, and flow stress
infographic.evidence_signal: At the same radiation dose, Frenkel-pair production follows NRSS > NLCO > NAA, while irradiation strongly degrades the AA model but has limited impact on strength and flow stress in RSS and LCO.
infographic.watch_item: The conclusions are based on atomistic modeling, so transferability to real alloys depends on how well the potentials and idealized chemical-order states represent experimental microstructures.
infographic.paper_title: Primary damage and mechanical degradation of WTaCrV refractory high-entropy alloy: effects of solid-solution and chemical ordering

title: When One Adapter Speaks for Many: Discovering Low-Rank Redundancy in Continual Fine-Tuning
authors: Tanguy Dieudonné, Giulia Lanzillotta, Enis Simsar, Louis Barinka, Thomas Hofmann
abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) has become the standard tool for parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large pretrained models. When applied sequentially across tasks in Continual Learning (CL), the standard assumption is that each new task requires a dedicated low-rank adapter. In this work, we challenge this assumption empirically and structurally. We show that task-specific LoRA adapters in CL exhibit significant low-rank redundancy: the subspaces spanned by adapters trained on different tasks substantially overlap, and in many cases earlier adapters can faithfully represent later tasks. Building on this observation, we propose LiteLoRA, a plug-and-play gating mechanism that learns at train time whether to recruit a new adapter or reuse existing low-rank representations. Our method reduces the number of active adapters by 20-70% while matching or exceeding state-of-the-art performance on standard CL benchmarks, revealing that structural redundancy is pervasive and that selective learning is sufficient to achieve stability without sacrificing plasticity.

infographic.key_insight: Task-specific LoRA adapters in continual fine-tuning are often redundant: their low-rank subspaces overlap enough that earlier adapters can sometimes represent later tasks.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For continual learning workflows, selective adapter reuse can cut memory and training overhead while preserving accuracy, making parameter-efficient adaptation more practical at scale.
method: Analyze overlap between LoRA subspaces learned on sequential tasks.
Introduce LiteLoRA, a train-time gating mechanism that chooses between recruiting a new adapter or reusing existing ones.
Reduce active adapters by selectively learning only when reuse is insufficient.
infographic.evidence_signal: Across standard continual learning benchmarks, LiteLoRA reduces the number of active adapters by 20–70% while matching or exceeding state-of-the-art performance.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on the gating policy and task similarity, so weak reuse decisions could still hurt adaptation on highly dissimilar tasks.
infographic.paper_title: When One Adapter Speaks for Many: Discovering Low-Rank Redundancy in Continual Fine-Tuning

title: Mesh-RL: Coupled subgrid reinforcement learning
authors: Behnam Gheshlaghi, Bahador Rashidi, Shahin Atakishiyev
abstract: Reinforcement learning in large or sparse-reward environments suffers from slow temporal-difference reward propagation, as value information spreads only locally across the state space. We propose Mesh-RL, a spatial domain-decomposition framework inspired by the finite element method and domain decomposition theory, which partitions the environment into overlapping subgrids and enforces boundary-consistent temporal-difference updates. Such an approach enables localized learning while ensuring globally coherent value propagation. Unlike hierarchical or model-based approaches, Mesh-RL accelerates long-range credit assignment without modifying the reward function, Bellman operator, or introducing explicit planning mechanisms. We evaluate Mesh-RL on hazard-dense grid-world environments with varying geometries and mesh resolutions. Across Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q, Mesh-RL consistently improves convergence speed, cumulative reward, and learning stability. Higher mesh resolutions sustain exploration, prevent premature convergence, and substantially accelerate value propagation to distant states. While Dyna-Q already benefits from internal planning, it still achieves additional gains under structured decomposition. Overall, Mesh-RL introduces a principled spatial domain-decomposition mechanism for accelerating temporal-difference learning. Our framework bridges finite element method-inspired boundary-consistency techniques from scientific computing with reinforcement learning to improve sample efficiency in sparse-reward environments. We will release source code of the study.

infographic.key_insight: Mesh-RL speeds up reinforcement learning by decomposing the environment into overlapping subgrids and enforcing boundary-consistent value updates so rewards propagate more globally.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The method imports finite-element/domain-decomposition ideas into RL, offering a principled way to improve sample efficiency in sparse-reward, large-state environments.
method: Partition the grid environment into overlapping subgrids.
Apply localized temporal-difference updates with boundary consistency across subgrids.
Use the decomposition with standard RL algorithms such as Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q.
infographic.evidence_signal: Evaluated on hazard-dense grid worlds with different geometries and mesh resolutions, Mesh-RL consistently improved convergence speed, cumulative reward, and learning stability across Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on choosing an appropriate mesh resolution and overlap; overly coarse or poorly tuned decomposition could reduce the benefit.
infographic.paper_title: Mesh-RL: Coupled subgrid reinforcement learning

title: CANNs: A Toolkit for Research on Continuous Attractor Neural Networks
authors: Sichao He, Aiersi Tuerhong, Shangjun She, Tianhao Chu, Yuling Wu, Junfeng Zuo
abstract: Continuous attractor neural networks (CANNs) are the canonical computational framework for how the brain encodes continuous variables such as spatial position, head direction, and movement direction, and explain the activity of hippocampal place cells, entorhinal grid cells, and head-direction cells. CANN research, however, is fragmented: most results rest on lab-specific implementations, general-purpose simulators lack CANN-specific abstractions, and the path from spike trains to attractor geometry in real recordings lacks a standardized toolkit. Here, we present a comprehensive open-source toolkit that unifies the full CANN research workflow. It combines three tightly integrated components: 1) canns, a Python library on BrainPy/JAX that provides standardized 1D/2D CANNs, spike-frequency-adaptation variants, grid cell networks, hierarchical path-integration models, and brain-inspired attractor architectures, together with curated datasets, task generators, an analyzer module and trainer modules for biologically plausible plasticity; 2) canns-lib, a Rust acceleration backend delivering hundreds-of-times speedups for spatial-navigation workloads and modest gains for Ripser-based persistent homology; 3) ASA (Attractor Structure Analyzer), a PySide6 pipeline applying persistent homology and cohomology to experimental neural recordings to detect ring-like and toroidal attractor signatures in real data. The toolkit ships with full-detail reproducible pipelines that recover recent CANN results including SFA-driven anticipative tracking, theta sweeps in head-direction/place/grid systems, and hierarchical path integration.

infographic.key_insight: The paper presents a unified open-source toolkit that standardizes modeling, simulation, acceleration, and analysis for continuous attractor neural network research across synthetic and experimental data.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It lowers the barrier to reproducible CANN studies by providing reusable JAX/BrainPy models, a fast Rust backend, and topology-based analysis tools for identifying attractor structure in neural recordings.
method: canns: standardized 1D/2D CANNs, SFA variants, grid-cell networks, hierarchical path-integration models, datasets, tasks, and trainers
canns-lib: Rust acceleration backend for spatial-navigation workloads and Ripser-based persistent homology
ASA: PySide6 pipeline using persistent homology/cohomology to detect ring-like and toroidal attractor signatures in real recordings
infographic.evidence_signal: The toolkit is validated by reproducing recent CANN results, including SFA-driven anticipative tracking, theta sweeps in head-direction/place/grid systems, and hierarchical path integration.
infographic.watch_item: Performance gains and topology-based signatures may depend on task structure, recording quality, and backend compatibility, so cross-dataset generalization should be checked carefully.
infographic.paper_title: CANNs: A Toolkit for Research on Continuous Attractor Neural Networks

title: Hydrogen segregation around a straight screw dislocation in bcc iron
authors: Margot Lucas, Marie Landeiro Dos Reis, Sylvain Queyreau, Xavier Feaugas
abstract: The interaction between hydrogen and screw dislocations in bcc iron is central to understanding hydrogen embrittlement. A major challenge lies in the high-dimensional parametric landscape governing this interaction. In this work, we perform a comprehensive set of molecular simulations using a reliable neural network interatomic potential, systematically exploring hydrogen binding across dislocation core structures (easy and hard cores), site types, and concentrations. From these energetics, we construct a thermodynamic framework that quantifies the statistical relevance of the various trapping configurations, thereby significantly reducing the complexity of the problem. Our results show good agreement with the limited density functional theory data available in the literature. We further delineate the validity domain of an elastic dipole description of hydrogen-dislocation interactions, providing a simplified yet physically grounded modeling approach. Finally, we demonstrate that the easy-core configuration plays a key role in rationalizing experimental hydrogen solubility limits. These findings establish a consistent multiscale foundation for incorporating hydrogen-dislocation interactions into larger-scale models of plasticity and embrittlement.

infographic.key_insight: A thermodynamic, multiscale treatment shows that hydrogen segregation around a screw dislocation in bcc iron is governed by a few statistically relevant trapping configurations, especially the easy-core state.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This provides a physically grounded way to model hydrogen-dislocation interactions for predicting hydrogen embrittlement and solubility limits in iron-based materials.
method: Used molecular simulations with a neural-network interatomic potential to scan dislocation core states, site types, and hydrogen concentrations.
Built a thermodynamic framework to rank trapping configurations by statistical relevance.
Assessed when an elastic dipole approximation is valid as a simplified interaction model.
infographic.evidence_signal: The simulation-based energetics were compared against the limited density functional theory data available in the literature and showed good agreement.
infographic.watch_item: The simplified elastic dipole description is only valid within a restricted domain, so applying it outside that range may miss important core-specific effects.
infographic.paper_title: Hydrogen segregation around a straight screw dislocation in bcc iron

title: hp-Optimal DG Approximation and Robust Schwarz Decompositions on One-Irregular Cubical Meshes
authors: Situan Li, Weiying Zheng
abstract: We study hp approximation and additive Schwarz decompositions for variable-order cubical finite element spaces on one-irregular meshes. For fitted homogeneous diffusion interface problems on one-irregular hexahedral meshes, we prove an hp-optimal energy-norm estimate for the interior penalty DG method. The interpolation input is a conforming hp interpolant obtained from fitted conforming closures of one-irregular vertex patches. We also derive stable decompositions for conforming and DG spaces. On one-irregular quadrilateral meshes the bounds allow locally comparable variable polynomial degrees and are independent of the mesh size, the local degrees, and, under a local coefficient quasi-monotonicity condition, the coefficient contrast. On one-irregular hexahedral meshes the conforming decomposition has the corresponding polylogarithmic loss; the DG-to-conforming reduction is used there for uniform-degree DG spaces. Numerical experiments illustrate the p-optimal DG error estimate and the robustness of the DG Schwarz preconditioner.

infographic.key_insight: The paper proves hp-optimal discontinuous Galerkin approximation and develops robust additive Schwarz decompositions for variable-order finite element spaces on one-irregular cubical meshes.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables efficient, scalable solvers and error-controlled simulations for heterogeneous diffusion/interface problems on adaptive 3D hexahedral meshes.
method: hp interpolation on fitted conforming closures of one-irregular vertex patches
Interior penalty DG error analysis for fitted homogeneous diffusion interface problems
Stable additive Schwarz decompositions for conforming and DG spaces, with DG-to-conforming reduction for uniform-degree 3D cases
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports theoretical hp-optimal energy-norm estimates and robustness bounds, and notes numerical experiments validating the DG error estimate and Schwarz preconditioner performance.
infographic.watch_item: Robustness in the 3D conforming decomposition includes a polylogarithmic loss, and coefficient-contrast independence requires a local quasi-monotonicity condition.
infographic.paper_title: hp-Optimal DG Approximation and Robust Schwarz Decompositions on One-Irregular Cubical Meshes

title: Parallel Manifold Steering: Efficient Adaptation of Large Associative Memories via Residual Energy Shaping
authors: Kanishk Awadhiya
abstract: Large Transformer models function as Dense Associative Memories (DAMs), retrieving knowledge via high-dimensional attractor dynamics driven by the self-attention mechanism \citep{ramsauer2020hopfield, wu2024attention}. However, adapting these frozen memory systems to new tasks presents a fundamental ``Plasticity-Stability'' dilemma. Current methods either risk catastrophic interference by modifying synaptic weights directly (e.g., LoRA) \citep{hu2021lora} or degrade associative capacity by clogging the retrieval buffer with static prompt tokens (e.g., VPT) \citep{jia2022vpt}. In this work, we propose \textbf{H-Res} (Hierarchical Residual Steering), a mechanism that modulates the effective energy landscape of the Transformer without altering its global equilibrium or expanding its sequence length. By formulating adaptation as a control problem on the activation manifold \citep{chen2018neuralode}, H-Res learns a state-dependent vector field that steers token trajectories into task-specific basins of attraction. We formally prove that H-Res preserves the attention entropy of the foundation model and facilitates Neural Collapse \citep{papyan2020prevalence}. Empirically, Manifold Steering outperforms global weight modification by 26\% on associative retrieval tasks and eliminates the computational overhead of prompt-based methods, scaling effectively to structured domains \citep{zha2023vtab}.

infographic.key_insight: H-Res adapts frozen Transformer memories by steering activations on the manifold, shaping the effective energy landscape without changing the model’s weights or sequence length.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This offers a low-overhead adaptation strategy for large pretrained models, relevant to deploying stable, task-specific AI systems in domains where preserving core model behavior matters.
method: Learn a state-dependent vector field over token activations.
Steer trajectories toward task-specific attractor basins in the residual/activation manifold.
Preserve global equilibrium and attention entropy while enabling adaptation.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports 26% better performance than global weight modification on associative retrieval tasks and claims better scalability to structured domains.
infographic.watch_item: The approach assumes the learned steering field generalizes across tasks and inputs, so implementation stability and robustness outside the reported retrieval settings should be validated.
infographic.paper_title: Parallel Manifold Steering: Efficient Adaptation of Large Associative Memories via Residual Energy Shaping

title: Analysis and Numerics of a Stationary Drift-Diffusion Model for Electrical Discharge in MEMS
authors: Runan He, Wenjia Xie
abstract: This work presents the analysis and numerical simulation of a stationary drift-diffusion model for electrical discharge in micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS). The model couples Poisson's equation for the electrostatic potential with continuity equations for positive ions and electrons, incorporating a Townsend-type impact ionization source term that depends exponentially on the electric field magnitude. We prove the existence of weak solutions under physically relevant assumptions and establish uniform bounds on the carrier densities. The proof relies on a regularization-approximation scheme with truncated nonlinearities, monotone operator theory (Browder-Minty), iterative energy estimates, and Stampacchia-type truncation arguments. We further develop a robust finite element solver to simulate the carrier density and electrostatic potential profiles for several geometries, including two-dimensional domains and a three-dimensional axisymmetric geometry.

infographic.key_insight: The paper proves well-posedness and demonstrates finite element simulation of a stationary drift-diffusion MEMS discharge model with exponentially field-dependent ionization.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It provides a mathematically grounded solver framework for predicting electrical breakdown and charge transport in MEMS devices.
method: Couple Poisson’s equation with continuity equations for positive ions and electrons.
Regularize and truncate nonlinear source terms, then apply Browder-Minty monotone operator arguments.
Use energy estimates and Stampacchia-type truncation to prove bounded weak solutions, then solve with FEM in 2D and axisymmetric 3D geometries.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports analytical existence and uniform boundedness results plus numerical simulations across several geometries, but no quantitative benchmark metrics.
infographic.watch_item: The Townsend-type exponential ionization term is highly nonlinear, so stability and accuracy may depend strongly on regularization and mesh resolution.
infographic.paper_title: Analysis and Numerics of a Stationary Drift-Diffusion Model for Electrical Discharge in MEMS
